title: Brioche à tête
subtitle: The fluted brioche in its fluted tin
excerpt: The classic French brioche, baked in a fluted brioche mould and distinguished by its bobbin-shaped topknot. The same butter-enriched dough as Nanterre, but shaped into a ball with a smaller ball pressed into its centre, the head that gives it its name.
sourceNotes: Brioche à tête is documented in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), and in earlier French culinary writing. The fluted tin and topknot shape are traditional Parisian boulangerie practice. The method here follows the same enriched dough as the Nanterre but with the individual fluted-mould format.

content.text: The dough is the same as for brioche Nanterre (same ratios, same slow butter addition, same cold overnight ferment). What changes is the shape: a large ball pressed into a fluted tin with a smaller ball seated in a well made in the centre. The
is the one part that needs practice, the join between the head and the body should be tight enough that the head doesn't lean or separate during baking.
Make the dough and cold ferment

Follow the same method as brioche Nanterre: mix flour, yeast, sugar, salt, eggs, and milk in a stand mixer with the dough hook, then incorporate the cold butter cube by cube over 15 minutes. Beat until smooth and shiny. Bulk ferment at room temperature for 2 hours, then refrigerate overnight (at least 6 hours).

Butter the fluted brioche tins generously. For 6 individual brioche (10 cm tins): divide the cold dough into 6 pieces (about 105 g each). From each piece, pinch off roughly one-fifth (about 20 g) and set aside. Roll the larger piece into a smooth ball and place in the tin. With two fingers, press a well into the centre of the ball going almost to the tin base. Roll the smaller piece into a tight oval (the head) and press its pointed end into the well. The head should sit proud of the tin.

Cover the tins loosely and proof at room temperature for 90 minutes. The dough should puff up to the rim of the tin and the head should look visibly risen. Do not overproof, the head starts to lean when the body over-expands.

Brush the surface and head carefully with beaten egg. Bake at 190°C conventional (170°C fan) for 18 to 22 minutes for individual brioche, or 28 to 32 minutes for a large one, until deep amber all over and an internal probe reads 93°C. If the head browns before the body is done, lay a piece of foil loosely over the top. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out.

recipe.yieldDescription: 6 individual brioche à tête (using 10 cm fluted tins) or 1 large (20 cm tin)
